evals.name: Open personal Chrome profile for YouTube posting
evals.prompt: Open my personal Chrome profile and go to YouTube Studio so I can create a post
evals.expected_output: Lists available Chrome profiles with their display names by running a bash command, then runs the correct `open -a 'Google Chrome'` command with `--profile-directory` pointing to the correct profile and navigates to studio.youtube.com

assertions.text: Response includes a bash code block that lists or reads Chrome profile directories (e.g., ls ~/Library/Application Support/Google/Chrome/ or reading Preferences files)
Response includes a bash or shell command using the --profile-directory flag when launching Chrome (e.g., open -a 'Google Chrome' ... --args --profile-directory=)
Response includes studio.youtube.com as the target URL in one of the provided commands or code blocks

evals.name: Create a YouTube community post
evals.prompt: Post a YouTube community update saying 'New video dropping Friday!' on my YouTube channel
evals.expected_output: Discovers Chrome profiles, opens Chrome with personal profile navigating to studio.youtube.com/community, provides AppleScript or bash to open the community post composer with the exact text, and includes a step to confirm submission

assertions.text: Response includes studio.youtube.com/community (not just studio.youtube.com) in a code block or command
Response includes the exact text 'New video dropping Friday!' somewhere in the provided code, AppleScript, or instructions
Response includes a code block to confirm the post was submitted — such as checking page state via JavaScript or reading the page title via AppleScript

evals.name: Upload a video file to YouTube
evals.prompt: Upload the video at ~/Desktop/tutorial.mp4 to YouTube using my personal profile
evals.expected_output: Provides a bash command to check ~/Desktop/tutorial.mp4 exists, opens Chrome with the personal profile at YouTube Studio, includes AppleScript or shell command to pass the file path to the upload dialog, and asks for or mentions setting video title/description

assertions.text: Response includes a bash code block checking whether ~/Desktop/tutorial.mp4 (or tutorial.mp4) exists (e.g., using [ -f ... ], ls, or test -f)
Response includes an AppleScript block or shell command that passes the file path to the OS file picker dialog (e.g., keystroke with the path, or a System Events block)
Response mentions video title and/or description — either asking the user to provide them or including placeholder fields for them
